# Ack

> A schema validation library for Dart and Flutter with a fluent runtime API,
> bidirectional codecs, and `@AckType()`-driven extension-type generation.

Ack validates external data with hand-written schemas built using the `Ack`
factory. Schemas are bidirectional: `parse` validates a boundary value and
produces a runtime value; `encode` is the inverse. When you want typed wrappers
over validated values, annotate top-level schema variables or getters with
`@AckType()` and run `ack_generator`.

## Packages

1. `ack`: core runtime validation library
2. `ack_annotations`: exposes `@AckType()`
3. `ack_generator`: generates extension types for annotated top-level schemas
4. `ack_firebase_ai`: converts Ack schemas to Firebase AI structured-output schemas

## Core runtime usage

```dart
import 'package:ack/ack.dart';

final userSchema = Ack.object({
  'name': Ack.string().minLength(2),
  'email': Ack.string().email(),
  'age': Ack.integer().min(0).optional(),
});

final result = userSchema.safeParse({
  'name': 'Alice',
  'email': 'alice@example.com',
});
```

## Codecs and encoding

A schema's parse direction validates a boundary value, decodes it, and validates
the runtime result. Its encode direction is the inverse: validate the runtime
value, encode it, and validate the boundary result.

`encode(value)` throws on failure; `safeEncode(value)` returns
`SchemaResult<Object>`. Both are available on every `AckSchema`.

```dart
final eventSchema = Ack.object({
  'name': Ack.string(),
  'startsAt': Ack.datetime(),
});

final event = eventSchema.parse({
  'name': 'Launch',
  'startsAt': '2026-05-04T10:00:00.000Z',
});
// event['startsAt'] is a DateTime

final payload = eventSchema.encode(event);
// payload['startsAt'] is back to '2026-05-04T10:00:00.000Z'
```

Define your own codec with `Ack.codec`:

```dart
final bigIntCodec = Ack.codec<String, BigInt>(
  Ack.string().matches(r'^-?\d+$'),
  Ack.instance<BigInt>(),
  decode: BigInt.parse,
  encode: (value) => value.toString(),
);
```

`Ack.instance<T>()` is the runtime side of a codec — it validates `value is T`
without any boundary-side coercion. Use it (or any other schema) as the second
argument to `Ack.codec`.

`SchemaEncodeError` is raised when encode fails. Common cases:

- `SchemaEncodeError.typeMismatch` — runtime value is the wrong type
- `SchemaEncodeError.requiredNotNull` — non-nullable schema received `null`
- one-way `.transform(...)` schemas have no encoder; calling `encode` fails
  with a message that points at `Ack.codec(...)`
- the user-supplied `encode` closure threw; the error is wrapped

Built-in transforms are bidirectional codecs and round-trip cleanly:
`Ack.date()` (`YYYY-MM-DD`), `Ack.datetime()` (UTC ISO-8601),
`Ack.uri()` (`Uri.toString()`), `Ack.duration()` (milliseconds),
enum schemas (encode emits `.name`).

`.transform(fn)` is one-way (parse only). It is now a `CodecSchema<T, R>` with
no encoder; reach for `Ack.codec(...)` when both directions are needed.

## No implicit primitive coercion

Primitive schemas validate runtime values as-is.

```dart
Ack.integer().safeParse('42'); // Fail — string is not int
```

Define an app-local codec at the boundary when conversion is needed:

```dart
final intStringCodec = Ack.codec<String, int>(
  Ack.string(),
  Ack.instance<int>(),
  decode: int.parse,
  encode: (value) => value.toString(),
);
intStringCodec.parse('42'); // 42
```

## Defaults

Defaults are a parse-only wrapper applied via `.withDefault(value)`. They never
synthesize boundary data during encode.

```dart
final roleSchema = Ack.string().withDefault('guest');
```

Pre-2.0 constructor defaults (`Ack.string(defaultValue: 'x')`) and built-in
implicit primitive coercion are removed. Migrate to `Ack.codec(...)` at the
boundary and `.withDefault(value)` for defaults.

## Deprecations

- `TransformedSchema<I, O>` is now a `@Deprecated` typedef alias for
  `CodecSchema<I, O>`. Type annotations continue to work; the previous
  positional constructor and `.schema` / `.transformer` fields are gone.
  Migrate to `CodecSchema(inputSchema: ..., outputSchema: ..., decoder: ...,
  encoder: ...)` with `.inputSchema` / `.outputSchema` / `.decoder` /
  `.encoder`. The alias will be removed in a future release.

## AckType generation

`@AckType()` is supported only on:

- top-level schema variables
- top-level schema getters

It is not supported on classes or instance members.

Example:

```dart
import 'package:ack/ack.dart';
import 'package:ack_annotations/ack_annotations.dart';

part 'user_schema.g.dart';

@AckType()
final addressSchema = Ack.object({
  'street': Ack.string(),
  'city': Ack.string(),
});

@AckType()
final userSchema = Ack.object({
  'name': Ack.string(),
  'address': addressSchema,
});
```

Generated capabilities:

- `UserType.parse(data)`
- `UserType.safeParse(data)`
- typed getters such as `String get name` and `AddressType get address`

## Supported AckType schema shapes

- `Ack.object(...)`
- `Ack.string()`
- `Ack.integer()`
- `Ack.double()`
- `Ack.boolean()`
- `Ack.list(...)`
- `Ack.literal(...)`
- `Ack.enumString(...)`
- `Ack.enumValues(...)`
- bidirectional codecs (`Ack.codec<I, O>(...)`) and built-in codec helpers such
  as `.transform<Uri>(...)`, `Ack.date()`, `Ack.datetime()`, `Ack.uri()`,
  `Ack.duration()`
- `Ack.discriminated(...)` with the constraints below

Not supported for extension-type generation:

- `Ack.any()`
- `Ack.anyOf()`
- transformed object schemas
- transformed discriminated schemas

## Discriminated AckType schemas

`Ack.discriminated(...)` works with `@AckType()` when:

- the base schema is a top-level `@AckType()` declaration
- `schemas` is a non-empty map literal
- each branch is a top-level schema variable/getter reference
- each branch is an `@AckType()` object schema
- each branch is non-nullable
- each branch is declared in the same library as the base
- each branch defines the discriminator field with `Ack.literal(...)`
- the literal value matches the branch key

Both JSON Schema emission paths (`toJsonSchema()` and `toJsonSchemaModel()`)
verify the literal-discriminator pattern and throw an `ArgumentError` if a
branch declares the discriminator with a literal whose value does not match
the branch label.

Example:

```dart
@AckType()
final catSchema = Ack.object({
  'type': Ack.literal('cat'),
  'lives': Ack.integer(),
});

@AckType()
final dogSchema = Ack.object({
  'type': Ack.literal('dog'),
  'breed': Ack.string(),
});

@AckType()
final petSchema = Ack.discriminated(
  discriminatorKey: 'type',
  schemas: {
    'cat': catSchema,
    'dog': dogSchema,
  },
);
```

## Resolution rules

AckType generation is intentionally strict:

- nested object fields must reference named top-level schemas
- inline anonymous object schemas are rejected for typed generation
- cross-file direct imports, prefixed imports, and re-exports are supported
- unannotated object schema references fail generation instead of silently falling back to raw maps
- circular schema alias/reference chains fail generation

## Runtime API reminders

- `schema.parse(data)` throws on invalid input; returns the runtime value
- `schema.safeParse(data)` returns `SchemaResult<T>` (no throw)
- `schema.encode(value)` throws on invalid runtime value; returns the boundary value
- `schema.safeEncode(value)` returns `SchemaResult<Object>` (no throw)
- `SchemaResult<T>.getOrThrow()` returns the validated value or throws `AckException`
- `.optional()` allows a field to be omitted
- `.nullable()` allows a present field to hold `null`
- `.withDefault(value)` supplies a parse-time default; encode never synthesizes defaults
- object schemas support `additionalProperties: true`

## Build command

```bash
dart run build_runner build --delete-conflicting-outputs
```
